title: C# SOLID — Paper 4
description: Covers OCP refactoring, LSP concepts, LSP refactoring, ISP concepts, ISP refactoring, Decorator Pattern, Observer Pattern, Repository Pattern, Code Smells & SOLID Violations, and Refactoring Techniques.

questions.text: ST08-1: Which of the following best describes the ISP refactoring approach of 'splitting interfaces'?

options.text: Merging two small interfaces into one larger interface for simplicity.
Breaking a fat interface into multiple smaller, role-specific interfaces so clients only depend on what they use.
Removing all interface abstractions and using concrete classes directly.
Replacing interfaces with abstract classes.

questions.text: ST15-9: In C#, which syntax is used to subscribe a method to an event?

options.text: event.Subscribe(handler)
event += handler
event.Add(handler)
event.Attach(handler)

questions.text: ST04-10: A notification system must support Email, SMS, and Push notifications. Applying OCP, what is the best design?

options.text: A NotificationService class with methods SendEmail(), SendSms(), and SendPush(), updated each time a new type appears.
An INotificationChannel interface implemented by EmailChannel, SmsChannel, and PushChannel, with new channels added as new classes.
A single SendNotification(string type, string message) method with a switch on type.
An enum NotificationType with values for each channel type.

questions.text: ST18-1: The 'Extract Method' refactoring technique is used to:

options.text: Move a method from one class to another.
Turn a code fragment in a long method into a separate named method.
Replace a method body with a call to a strategy object.
Remove a method that is no longer needed.

questions.text: ST16-7: A repository method `GetOrdersByCustomer(int customerId)` returns a `List<Order>`. Which concern does this violate if business rules are also placed inside the repository method?

options.text: ISP — the repository interface has too many methods.
SRP — the repository mixes data-access logic with business logic.
OCP — the repository cannot be extended.
LSP — the return type is too specific.

questions.text: ST06-8: Which of the following is a valid technique for fixing an LSP violation caused by an incorrect inheritance hierarchy?

options.text: Add null checks throughout the codebase that uses the base type.
Flatten the hierarchy and use composition instead of inheriting from the problematic base class.
Mark the violating override method as sealed.
Add a type-check (is/as) before each polymorphic call.

questions.text: ST17-5: A 'Long Method' code smell indicates a violation of which SOLID principle?

options.text: OCP — the method cannot be extended.
SRP — the method is doing too many things and should be broken up.
DIP — the method depends on concrete types.
ISP — the method is part of a fat interface.

questions.text: ST05-10: Which of the following statements about LSP and type hierarchies is correct?

options.text: An 'is-a' relationship in domain terms always guarantees LSP compliance in code.
An 'is-a' relationship in domain terms does not guarantee LSP compliance; behavioral substitutability must also hold.
LSP only applies to abstract classes, not interfaces.
LSP compliance is only required for sealed classes.

questions.text: ST07-5: An `IMultiFunctionDevice` interface declares Print(), Scan(), Fax(), and Copy(). A SimplePrinter class implements it but throws NotSupportedException for Scan(), Fax(), and Copy(). Which principle is violated?

options.text: SRP
OCP
LSP
ISP

questions.text: ST14-8: A `CachingRepository` wraps a real `IRepository` and returns cached results. This is an example of which pattern?

options.text: Proxy (or Decorator)
Singleton
Adapter
Facade

questions.text: ST08-2: After splitting a fat IWorker interface into ITaskWorker and IBreakWorker, a Robot class should:

options.text: Implement both ITaskWorker and IBreakWorker regardless of capability.
Implement only ITaskWorker, since robots do not take breaks.
Implement neither interface and use a concrete base class.
Implement ITaskWorker and throw an exception in IBreakWorker methods.

questions.text: ST18-2: 'Replace Conditional with Polymorphism' is a refactoring that:

options.text: Removes all if/else and switch statements from a codebase.
Replaces type-checking conditional branches with overridden methods in subclasses or strategy implementations.
Converts all enum-based logic into lambda expressions.
Merges multiple conditional methods into one large method.

questions.text: ST04-11: OCP is often said to be achieved 'parameterically' in functional programming through:

options.text: Switch expressions
Higher-order functions (passing behaviour as parameters)
Immutable data structures
Pattern matching

questions.text: ST05-11: A unit test expects that calling `Calculate(animal)` returns a positive number for any `Animal`. A `VirtualAnimal` subclass always returns -1. This test failure demonstrates:

options.text: A DIP violation — the test depends on a concrete type.
An LSP violation — VirtualAnimal breaks the behavioral contract of Animal.
An SRP violation — the test tests two things at once.
An OCP violation — the test was not extended for VirtualAnimal.

questions.text: ST07-6: Which of the following is an ISP-compliant refactoring of a fat IAnimal interface?

options.text: Combine IAnimal with IVehicle for maximum reuse.
Split IAnimal into IFlyable, ISwimmable, and IWalkable, each implemented only by animals that perform those actions.
Add default implementations to IAnimal so implementors don't need to override everything.
Replace IAnimal with an abstract class Animal.

questions.text: ST16-8: In a Domain-Driven Design context, which layer is responsible for calling repository methods?

options.text: The presentation layer (controllers/views).
The application/domain service layer.
The infrastructure layer directly.
The database migration scripts.

questions.text: ST06-9: In C#, the `IEnumerable<T>` and `ICollection<T>` hierarchy is an example of a partial LSP concern. Why might code passing `IEnumerable<T>` be safer than passing `ICollection<T>`?

options.text: IEnumerable<T> has more methods, giving callers more options.
IEnumerable<T> is read-only; passing it avoids mutations that callers may not expect.
ICollection<T> implementations always violate LSP.
IEnumerable<T> is always faster at runtime.

questions.text: ST17-6: Which of the following refactoring patterns directly addresses a 'God Class' code smell?

options.text: Inline Class
Extract Class and Move Method to redistribute responsibilities
Introduce Explaining Variable
Replace Magic Number with Symbolic Constant

questions.text: ST18-3: 'Extract Class' is appropriate when:

options.text: A class is too small and has fewer than three fields.
A subset of a class's fields and methods naturally cluster together and form a cohesive concept of their own.
A class has too many tests.
Two classes share an identical method that should be centralised.

questions.text: ST15-10: When using EventHandler in C#, what is the purpose of the `sender` parameter?

options.text: It holds the event data payload.
It provides a reference to the object that raised the event, allowing subscribers to act on or query the publisher.
It determines the priority of the event handler.
It specifies whether the event is synchronous or asynchronous.

questions.text: ST08-3: In C#, a class may implement multiple interfaces. How does this support ISP?

options.text: It allows the class to be registered as multiple service types in a DI container.
It enables a class to satisfy multiple role-specific contracts without forcing clients to depend on a single fat interface.
It causes C# to generate efficient vtable entries for each interface.
It reduces the number of assemblies needed.

questions.text: ST14-9: Applying the Decorator pattern to a stream in .NET — e.g., `new GZipStream(new BufferedStream(new FileStream(...)))` — demonstrates:

options.text: The Composite pattern, because streams form a tree structure.
The Decorator pattern, where each stream wraps another to add behaviour (buffering, compression) without modifying Stream.
The Builder pattern, constructing a complex stream step by step.
The Adapter pattern, converting one stream interface to another.

questions.text: ST04-12: Robert Martin's OCP states that a module should be both 'open' and 'closed' simultaneously. How is this paradox resolved?

options.text: By having two versions of each class: an open version and a closed version.
By separating the stable abstraction (interface/abstract class) that is closed from the extension points (implementations) that are open.
By using feature flags to enable or disable behavior at runtime.
By keeping source code private and only distributing compiled binaries.

questions.text: ST06-10: The Design by Contract concept of 'class invariant' in the context of LSP means:

options.text: The class must never change at runtime.
Conditions that must hold true for all instances of a class throughout their lifetime; subclasses must preserve base class invariants.
The class must have an invariant number of properties.
Constants defined within the class.

questions.text: ST17-7: 'Primitive Obsession' is a code smell describing:

options.text: Using too many loops in a single method.
Overusing primitive types (int, string) for domain concepts that should be represented as small value objects or classes.
A class that has only primitive fields.
Using bitwise operations for business logic.

questions.text: ST07-7: Which principle is most directly supported by ISP?

options.text: High cohesion — interfaces should group only related operations that clients actually need.
Low coupling — every class should depend on every other class.
High coupling — interfaces should aggregate all possible operations.
Low cohesion — interfaces should group unrelated operations for convenience.

questions.text: ST16-9: Which of the following is a disadvantage of the Repository pattern?

options.text: It couples domain logic directly to the ORM.
It can add unnecessary abstraction layers for simple CRUD applications, increasing boilerplate code.
It prevents the use of dependency injection.
It makes unit testing harder.

questions.text: ST18-4: 'Move Method' refactoring is appropriate when:

options.text: A method is called from many different classes.
A method uses more features of another class than it does of its own class, suggesting it belongs there.
A method is too long and needs to be split.
A method has too many parameters.

questions.text: ST08-4: In C#, the ISP is often applied when defining service contracts. Which approach best applies ISP to a command/query split?

options.text: One IService interface with all read and write methods.
Separate IReadRepository<T> and IWriteRepository<T> interfaces so read-only consumers don't depend on mutation methods.
A single abstract class with virtual read and write methods.
An enum-based service locator choosing read or write operations.

questions.text: ST05-12: An interface `IShape` defines `Area()`. A class `Line` implements `IShape` and returns 0 for `Area()`. Is this LSP compliant?

options.text: No — returning 0 is always an LSP violation.
Yes — if the contract of Area() allows returning 0 for degenerate shapes and callers are prepared for this.
Yes — but only if Line is a sealed class.
No — Line should not implement IShape because it is not a 2D shape.
